one_line_takeaway: Roblox Guard 1.0, an 8B Llama-3.1 LoRA fine-tune trained on 384K examples (public safety datasets plus a 3-stage synthetic pipeline with CoT and input-inversion augmentation), sets new state-of-the-art F1 on several public guardrail benchmarks and dramatically outperforms existing guardrails (3.5-66.3% F1) on the paper's new fine-grained RobloxGuard-Eval benchmark, where its own 79.6% F1 highlights that other guardrails fail to generalize to platform-specific, non-saturated safety taxonomies.
key_finding: Roblox Guard 1.0 achieves state-of-the-art or comparable F1 across most prompt- and response-level benchmarks versus larger/commercial baselines: 91.9% on Aegis 1.0 Prompt (vs. GPT-4o 83.2%, LlamaGuard3 74.8%), 89.5% on WildGuard Prompt (vs. GPT-4o 87.9%), 79.1% on Toxic Chat (vs. next-best BingoGuard 75.7%, GPT-4o 68.1%), 87.3% on BeaverTails (vs. GPT-4o 83.8%, LlamaGuard3 69.7%). The largest reported gap is on RobloxGuard-Eval (the paper's own new benchmark): Roblox Guard 1.0 scores 79.6% F1 while all baseline guardrail models collapse (LlamaGuard3 3.5%, WildGuard-7B 15.1%, NemoGuard-8B 23.6%, BingoGuard-8B 25.9%, ShieldGemma-7B 55.5%, GPT-4o 66.3%), which the authors present as evidence that existing public benchmarks are saturated and that generalization to Roblox's fine-grained, platform-specific taxonomy is a distinguishing capability. Ablations (Table 6) show: removing synthetic data drops RobloxGuard-Eval F1 from 79.6% to 20.3% (and fails to produce valid inferences on HarmBench entirely), and drops OAI Mod from 70.3% to 49.2% and WildGuard Response from 80.6% to 68.3% -- identified as the single largest contribution to domain-specific performance. Removing CoT rationales degrades Aegis 2.0 Response by 4.4 points and HarmBench by 3.9 points (but slightly improves SafeRLHF and RobloxGuard-Eval, attributed to those benchmarks containing more 'straightforward' violations where pattern matching suffices). Removing input inversion most hurts XSTest (-3.0 points) and WildGuard Response (-2.6 points), attributed to reduced robustness to diverse/adversarial instruction formats.
core_contribution: The paper (Roblox) introduces Roblox Guard 1.0, an instruction fine-tuned LLM guardrail model built on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct that performs input (prompt) and output (response) content moderation, and argues for a 'taxonomy-adaptive' guardrail paradigm that generalizes to unseen/custom safety taxonomies at inference time rather than being retrained per taxonomy. The model is fine-tuned with LoRA (rank r=16) on a training corpus of 384,233 examples combining three public datasets (Aegis, WildGuard, BeaverTails) with synthetically generated data (from Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-v0.1, and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-Abliterated-v2 as response generators, judged/labeled by Mistral-Small-24B-Instruct-2501 and DeepSeek-R1), augmented with chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales and an 'input inversion' technique that permutes the ordering of output components (CoT, label, category) during training to avoid output-format overfitting. The paper also releases RobloxGuard-Eval, a new open-sourced benchmark of 2,872 human red-teamed and expert-labeled prompt-response pairs spanning 25 (23 populated) fine-grained content-safety categories drawn from Roblox's platform-specific policy taxonomy (e.g., donation solicitation, off-platform advertising, jailbreaking of Roblox systems), intended to be less saturated than existing public guardrail benchmarks.
threat_model: The paper does not model an adversary attacking the guardrail model itself (e.g., no adversarial robustness/attack-evasion analysis of RobloxGuard). Its safety scope is: LLM-based conversational systems producing outputs that violate a content-safety policy, where inputs may include adversarial or jailbreak-style prompts (sourced/generated via an 'AI security tester' synthetic-data role that devises 'jailbreak techniques' to elicit policy-violating target-LLM responses for training-data construction). The guardrail's job is binary classification (violation / no violation) of prompts and prompt+response pairs against a taxonomy of harm categories (e.g., child exploitation, terrorism, hate speech, self-harm, sexual content, scams, platform-misuse/jailbreaking of Roblox systems). This is a content-moderation/safety-classification threat model, not a model-security (prompt-injection defense evasion, guardrail-bypass attack) threat model in the adversarial-ML sense.
limitations: The paper does not evaluate the guardrail model's own adversarial robustness (i.e., whether an attacker can craft inputs to evade RobloxGuard's classification), so it makes no claims about guardrail-bypass resistance despite training partly on jailbreak-style synthetic prompts. Judge-model labels for the synthetic training data were calibrated against GPT-4o (F1=85.61%, FPR=9.34%) rather than against full human review of the entire synthetic corpus, so synthetic-label noise is bounded but not eliminated. RobloxGuard-Eval's category distribution is highly imbalanced (1,980 'None' examples out of 2,872 total; several violation categories have fewer than 10 examples, e.g., Intellectual Property Violations n=1, Paid Random Items n=2), which the paper does not explicitly caveat as limiting per-category statistical reliability. The CoT ablation shows CoT sometimes hurts performance (SafeRLHF, RobloxGuard-Eval), which the paper explains post hoc rather than predicting. The evaluation is restricted to F1 as the sole reported metric (no precision/recall breakdown, no calibration/latency-vs-accuracy tradeoff analysis beyond one latency figure), and taxonomy definitions/policy text used at inference are Roblox's own, so 'taxonomy-adaptive' generalization is demonstrated mainly via zero-shot transfer to other published benchmarks' taxonomies rather than via a systematic study of adaptation to genuinely novel, unseen third-party taxonomies.

datasets_used: WildGuard (48,783 prompt-only + 37,934 prompt+response training examples; WildGuardTest 1.7K used as test set)
BeaverTails (99,481 prompt+response training examples; full test set used for evaluation)
Llama Synthetic (53,840 examples, generated via Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct as response generator)

RobloxGuard-Eval (new, released by this paper: 2,872 examples, 23 populated safety categories out of a 25-category taxonomy, human red-teamed prompts, expert-labeled with 2-of-3 annotator agreement required)
HarmBench (510 instances; 602 text-behavior classifier values used)
quantitative_results: Training corpus total: 384,233 examples (213,289 positive, 170,944 negative)
Aegis 1.0 Prompt F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 91.9% vs. GPT-4o 83.2%, LlamaGuard3-8B 74.8%, WildGuard-7B 89.4%, ShieldGemma-7B 88.7%, BingoGuard-8B 90.4%, NemoGuard-8B 89.8%
WildGuard Prompt F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 89.5% vs. GPT-4o 87.9%, LlamaGuard3-8B 70.1%
Toxic Chat F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 79.1% vs. next-best BingoGuard-8B 75.7%, LlamaGuard3-8B 50.9%
BeaverTails F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 87.3% vs. GPT-4o 83.8%, LlamaGuard3-8B 69.7%
RobloxGuard-Eval F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 79.6% vs. LlamaGuard3-8B 3.5%, WildGuard-7B 15.1%, NemoGuard-8B 23.6%, BingoGuard-8B 25.9%, ShieldGemma-7B 55.5%, GPT-4o 66.3%
SafeRLHF F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 69.9% (highest among compared models)
XSTest F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 86.4%, lower than BingoGuard-8B (94.9%) and WildGuard-7B (94.4%)
OAI Mod F1: Roblox Guard 1.0 70.3%, lower than ShieldGemma-7B (82.1%) and BingoGuard-8B (77.9%)

Ablation: removing synthetic data drops RobloxGuard-Eval from 79.6% to 20.3% F1; drops OAI Mod from 70.3% to 49.2%; drops WildGuard Response from 80.6% to 68.3%; model produces no valid HarmBench inferences without synthetic data
Ablation: removing CoT drops Aegis 2.0 Response by 4.4 points (86.0% to 81.6%) and HarmBench by 3.9 points (85.7% to 81.8%); RobloxGuard-Eval improves slightly (79.6% to 82.3%) without CoT
Ablation: removing input inversion drops XSTest by 3.0 points (86.4% to 83.4%) and WildGuard Response by 2.6 points (80.6% to 78.0%)
Judge-model calibration: GPT-4o vs. human expert ground truth achieved F1=85.61%, FPR=9.34%, recall=90.36%
Inference latency: 869.9ms average over 10 runs for a 790-token payload (770 prompt + 20 completion tokens) on AWS g6.12xlarge via vLLM
Roblox Guard 1.0 training set size (384,233) compared to WildGuard (86,759), BingoGuard (54,897), NemoGuard-8B (30,763)
